# Transcrição: 05- Report Setup & Analysis- Funnel KPIs 

- **Fonte:** 05- Report Setup & Analysis- Funnel KPIs 
- **Duração:** 07:31
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

So this video here, same ad account as I've shared for the top level KPIs, is another useful kind of view that I like to look at is my funnel KPIs. So this is a standard Shopify store and it has the standard events installed. Now this is the way I like to look at it. I like to look at it top down. And what I mean by that is I want to look at my cost per purchase as my kind of top level metric that has the most meaning. But then I look backwards at the steps before that. So prior to cost per purchase is cost per payment info, checkout initiated, ad to cart, search, complete reg and all the way down to content view, landing page, outbound click. So with this particular view, what I'm looking at is how well is the funnel performing. So right now I'm in the hot part of the funnel, which is retargeting. We currently have five ad sets running. So I can see, you know, this one here, for example, is only a 1.53 euros, $38 cost per purchase for retargeting, which is not great. But where are we falling down? So I can go all the way back to my cost per unique ad to cart. Now, one thing I will say about looking at metrics is, you know, sometimes you will have user behavior where people are viewing multiple products or they're adding to cart multiple times. And why that's important to understand is if I look at my metrics, so let's let's move past the cost per and let's look at the hard metrics. So here's the story. So on average, actually for the state period, I've had 13,857 people come out of the ad account and click into my landing page that's been recorded. I've had six registrations completed. There's a separate campaign for this 347 searches, or unique and 503 total searches. And then you look at how many people viewed my product page. Now if I looked at content views and that's the pixel event for view content that's firing my product page, I might look at this and say, wow, that's amazing. I've had nearly 30,000 people or 30,000 views of my product page. The reality is only 8,000 of them were unique. So that means that for example, on average for this particular store, people are looking at three to four products per visit because the view content pixels firing three to four times every time they're there. So that gives me an idea of what the user is doing their intent. This is a multi product e-com store sells apparel. So it makes sense that people are browsing through products. And then I look at add to carts 5,300 add to carts of which only 1,700 of them are unique.

## [03:00 - 06:00]

Now here's the thing. I'm real big on conversion rate optimization. If I'm only getting 1,700 clicks from the 8,000 people that viewed my page, that gives me an idea of what the conversion rate is from one page to the next. And I can use that over time and I can compare that across my different campaigns, my ad sets and ads to figure out what's working well and what's not working so well. So for example, this is 20 something percent. So 20 something percent, let's say 20 percent, just keep it easy. 20 percent of unique content views resulted in a unique add to cart. On average, people are adding to cart 4 to 5 times. So that's an interesting thing for me to think about. And this actually plays in with the average order value and how many people are adding to the cart because more products are being viewed. And from those different product pages, people are adding to cart. That's great. Then I look down and I say, right, how many checkouts were initiated? How many people went to payment info? How many people purchased? Now you might look at this and correctly say, well, you know, what's what's going on here if you've only had 374 people hitting this page, yet 865 people purchased. Let me tell you this is because there is PayPal on the site. PayPal bypasses this page. So this is only for people that pay by card, I use Stripe. For other people that bypass, they go into PayPal, which is not recorded here. And then they hit this page. So just as an FYI, that's for the data. So now I can kind of see the final metrics. Let's have a look at the cost and see how they look. So for the last 30 days, for this hot part of the funnel, we spent 10K, got 4.72 ROS 11 cost per acquisition. But if we work backwards and we look at cost per acquisition, cost per initiate checkout, unique add to cart, we can see where the differences are. So actually I'm looking here and saying for 14 days, which has the highest ROS performer of people that have added to cart within like zero to 14 days is the highest performance. The cost per unique add to cart and initiate checkout is very similar for those that are actually 14 to 28 days. So just think about if someone's added to cart in the first 14 days, we remark it with certain messages. If it's taken 14 days or longer, we remark it with different messages. But the cost up until this point of the funnel is actually very similar. In fact, 14 to 28 days cheaper and getting them into the add to cart stage is also fairly similar price. Cost per purchase is only marginally a couple of dollars difference. But look at the ROS difference here. So that's just something which you look at this and you think, right, what's the reason for this? What's going on?

## [06:00 - 07:31]

It could be average order value. It could be that more people are spending more closer to the actual first time of visit or maybe there's something else going on. But these are now the questions I can answer or at least ask myself because I have the data set up. So in this particular view, I've created funnel KPIs. So in my first block, I'm looking at the cost per X purchase, etc. In the second block, I'm looking at the actual uniques. So in this particular view, I can see the cost per and also the unique numbers. The reason why you want to look at uniques as well is sometimes you get accounts, campaigns, ads that haven't spent much, but the ROS looks really good, all the metrics look good. And then you look at the sales and what's actually being generated, the numbers are not that big. So it gives you an idea of how effective you are at performing as well. So let's just take that back out. So again, looking at my overall funnel metrics, I can quickly scan. For example, for me, I know that looking at unique cost per add to cart is a strong metric to look at overall performance. And I can compare that across different campaigns and I can drill down to the ad set and ad level. But having this kind of funnel view helps to make that decision making so much easier. And my goal is to create questions. I want to question my campaigns, my ads, my ad set performance, because those questions will come up with test ideas. And those test ideas are the things that will shift my performance.
